coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot-nanocomposite hydrogel as Denovo Nexus in rapid chondrogenesis
coredata.dc:creator: Majood, Misba
Agrawal, Omnarayan
Garg, Piyush
Selvam, Abhyavartin
Yadav, Sunil Kumar
Singh, Sonu
Kalyansundaram, Dinesh
Verma, Yogesh Kumar
Nayak, Ranu
Mohanty, Sujata
Mukherjee, Monalisa
coredata.dc:description: The incapability of cartilage to naturally regenerate and repair chronic muscular injuries urges the development of competent bionic rostrums. There is a need to explore faster strategies for chondrogenic engineering using mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs). Along these lines, rapid chondrocyte differentiation would benefit the transplantation demand affecting osteoarthritis (OA) and rheumatoid arthritis (RA) patients. In this report, a de novo nanocomposite was constructed by integrating biogenic carbon quantum dot (CQD) filler into synthetic hydrogel prepared from dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate (DMAEMA) and acrylic acid (AAc). The dominant structural integrity of synthetic hydrogel along with the chondrogenic differentiation potential of garlic peel derived CQDs led to faster chondrogenesis within 14 days. By means of extensive chemical and morphological characterization techniques, we illustrate that the hydrogel nanocomposite possesses lucrative features to influence rapid chondrogenesis. These results were further corroborated by bright field imaging, Alcian blue staining and Masson trichome staining. Thus, this stratagem of chondrogenic engineering conceptualizes to be a paragon in clinical wound care for the rapid manufacturing of chondrocytes.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The incapability of cartilage to naturally regenerate and repair chronic muscular injuries urges the development of competent bionic rostrums. There is a need to explore faster strategies for chondrogenic engineering using mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs). Along these lines, rapid chondrocyte differentiation would benefit the transplantation demand affecting osteoarthritis (OA) and rheumatoid arthritis (RA) patients. In this report, a de novo nanocomposite was constructed by integrating biogenic carbon quantum dot (CQD) filler into synthetic hydrogel prepared from dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate (DMAEMA) and acrylic acid (AAc). The dominant structural integrity of synthetic hydrogel along with the chondrogenic differentiation potential of garlic peel derived CQDs led to faster chondrogenesis within 14 days. By means of extensive chemical and morphological characterization techniques, we illustrate that the hydrogel nanocomposite possesses lucrative features to influence rapid chondrogenesis. These results were further corroborated by bright field imaging, Alcian blue staining and Masson trichome staining. Thus, this stratagem of chondrogenic engineering conceptualizes to be a paragon in clinical wound care for the rapid manufacturing of chondrocytes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The impairment of articular cartilage tissue is a common clinical diagnosis, often caused by accidental trauma, sports-related injuries, or inflammatory conditions like osteoarthritis (OA) and rheumatoid arthritis (RA). The main components of cartilage tissue are water, chondrocytes, type II collagen, and proteoglycans wherein water accounts for up to 60–80 % of the extracellular matrices (ECM) found in cartilage [1,2]. Cartilage regeneration potential of damaged cartilage tissue is constrained due to the absence of blood vessels and nerves within the cartilage tissue [3]. Presently, the available approaches for the restoration of cartilage tissue entail the transplantation of either cartilage tissue or chondrocytes. Nonetheless, the cartilage generated through these techniques fails to replicate the tissue architecture of native cartilage, and frequently exhibits inadequate mechanical characteristics to satisfy physiological demands [3].

Tissue engineering techniques offer alternative approaches to the traditional therapies by replacing damaged or diseased anatomical structures with newly cultivated tissues that are designed to meet the specific needs of individual patients and their respective sites of injury or disease [4,5]. In the quest for effective tissue engineering solutions, much attention has been devoted to the methods used for creating and improving porous 3D polymeric scaffolds. The complex structure and chemical properties of 3D scaffold matrices play a crucial role in providing mechanical support for the cells and influencing tissue growth [6–8]. To achieve a more precise replication of the intricate structures found in the natural ECM, scaffolds are fabricated using nanofibers, nanoparticles, and hydrogels. The use of hydrogels in biomedical applications is attractive owing to their high level of hydration, which closely mimics the ECM. The incorporation of cells into the three-dimensional framework of a hydrogel enables both structural reinforcement and the exchange of vital substances such as oxygen, nutrients, and metabolites [9,10]. The porosity of hydrogels is a crucial factor in promoting the diffusion of nutrients and oxygen, particularly when a fully functional vascular system is not present [11,12]. Interestingly, studies have highlighted the significance of hydrogels in the process of cartilage regeneration [13–15]. Notably, polymeric composites that incorporate nanotechnology provide promising avenues for material exploration, owing to their vast potential in diverse applications. The integration of nanoparticles with hydrogels holds great potential as a viable approach for tissue regeneration [16,17]. Nanoparticles, which can be made of organic/polymeric and inorganic materials, act as reinforcing agents for hydrogels.

The unique attributes of nanomaterials, stemming from their notable differences in physicochemical properties compared to the bulk phase, can be harnessed to design polymeric nanocomposites with tunable mechanical and biological properties. The nanoscale size increases the surface area to volume ratio, leading to enhanced surface reactivity, release of loaded bioactive agents, bioavailability, and biocompatibility. Addition of nano filler can enhance various material properties such as elasticity, crystallinity, and porosity [18–20].

Quantum dots (QDs) have superior optical properties and can be utilized in a biomedical arena. However, one of the limitations associated with semiconductor metal quantum dots pertains to their elevated toxicity, which can be attributed to the utilization of heavy metals during their fabrication process. The toxicity of heavy metals, even at lower levels, has been widely recognized, rendering them less appropriate for clinical investigations as they pose a threat to human beings and simultaneously hamper their practical applications. QD-based nanosystems have gained significant attentions in recent years due to their distinctive structures and properties, which make them potential materials for biomedical applications [21]. QDs possess numerous advantages that render them as optimal contenders for tissue engineering applications, encompassing low toxicity, remarkable biodegradability, biocompatibility, mechanical robustness, antimicrobial properties, and biomineralization capabilities, alongside their utilization in real-time tissue monitoring and visualization [22,23]. It has been indicated that the hydrogels incorporating QDs exhibit enhanced cell proliferation and migration within scaffolds, heightened osteogenic differentiation, augmented tissue granulation, and increased collagen deposition during the tissue regeneration process. This is possibly because of large number of surface functional groups (such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino) that have the potential to be readily conjugated with other components to enhance tissue regeneration [24,25]. The integration of QDs into scaffolds can serve as a cell culture substrate, guiding cell growth along the nanofibers of the scaffold and enhancing cellular activity [26]. This suggests that the vast array of functional groups on the surface of QDs play a crucial role in facilitating cellular activities. Current research primarily centers around the design and implementation of QDs and scaffold materials, with minimal consideration given to the mechanism of action that underlies their biological effects [23,27]. However, CQDs exhibit high biocompatibility, possess a hydrophilic surface, exhibit chemical stability, and demonstrate favorable compatibility with cellular systems [28]. CQDs also facilitate the upregulation of numerous regulatory factors that play a crucial role in dictating the destiny of stem cells. Due to their capacity to guide stem cell differentiation and in vivo tracking, they can serve as a cost-effective substitute for costly induction factors [16]. The present investigation involves the synthesis of a nanocomposite and investigating its impact on stem cell differentiation into chondrocytes. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report for rapid chondrocyte differentiation on a hydrogel nanocomposite achieved within a short timeframe. It is interesting to note that we observed chondrocyte differentiation of cells grown over nanocomposite within 14 days of induction, while cells grown over neat hydrogel and tissue culture plate required 18 days for complete differentiation. We believe that this emerging technology has the potential to open new frontiers in the field of tissue regeneration.

The observed characteristic of nanocomposites may be ascribed to the utilization of CQDs, which stimulate chondrogenesis, and hydrogel, which amplifies chondrogenesis. Possibly owing to their synergistic effects aids in rapid chondrogenesis thereby decreasing chondrocyte differentiation period from 18 to 14 days.

Garlic peel, an agrowaste was purchased from numerous vegetable vendors in Uttar Pradesh, India, for CQD synthesis. The “Institutional Animal Ethics Committee” (Approval No: CPCSEA/AIP/2015/03/005) at the Amity Institute of Pharmacy, Amity University in Noida, Uttar Pradesh, endorsed the in vivo animal model experimental protocol for the current study. This was conducted in accordance with the guidelines established by the “Committee for the Purpose of Control and Supervision of Experiments on Animals” (CPCSEA; Ministry of Environment, Forest, and Climate Change). For subsequent research, cryopreserved hWJ-MSCs (n = 3) were revived in low-glucose Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM-LG: Gibco) with 10 % FBS and 1 % PenStrep.

Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, United States) supplied 98 % pure Dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate (DMAEMA) and 99 % pure acrylic acid (AAc) for nanocomposite synthesis. TEMED was acquired from SRL Pvt. Ltd., India. The procurement of ammonium persulphate (APS) from Qualigen Fine Chemicals, India. All reactions and preparations were conducted in double-deionized water and for the preparation of buffer solutions, India Pharmacopoeia, 1996, II standards were used.

For CQD synthesis, already established lab protocol was followed [29]. Briefly, CQDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal process conducted in a typical laboratory setting. To eliminate impurities, the garlic peel was extensively washed in double-distilled water. The washed peels were dehydrated in a hot-air oven at 60 °C until it was completely desiccated. After being treated with liquid nitrogen, the desiccated peels were subsequently crushed into a powder. Following which 0.5 g of garlic peel powder was sonicated in 15 mL double distilled water, and then precursor was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (PTFE)-lined 40 mL stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave is then heated at 180 °C for 4 h while under autogenous pressure. The dark black crude product was washed with deionized water and filtered through grade 4 Gooch crucibles. The filtrate was sonicated for 2 h and dried in a hot air-oven to obtain CQDs.

The X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by utilizing an M/S Philips X'pert Pro instrument that emitted Cu Kα radiation and were recorded within the 2θ range of 5–45°. The specimens underwent an initial swelling process in a pH 7.4 phosphate buffer solution (PBS) followed by lyophilization using a freeze dryer (Ilshin Lab., Co., Ltd.) for a duration of 24 h. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEOL, JEM-2100F electron microscope with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The samples were prepared through the process of drop casting the material onto a copper grid coated with carbon, followed by drying at ambient temperature. The examination of the morphology of hydrogels was conducted through the utilization of scanning electron microscopy (SEM), specifically the ZEISS EVO 50 model. To conduct SEM analysis, the fully swollen samples were subjected to lyophilization. Subsequently, they were affixed onto an aluminum stub with the aid of double-sided adhesive conducting carbon tape. The samples were then subjected to gold coating in a PolaronE 5100 Gold Sputter Coating unit.

Raman spectra were acquired utilizing a Raman spectrometer that was coupled with a microscope (Varian 7000 FT-Raman and Varian 600 UMA). The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) analysis was conducted utilizing an ATR-FTIR model Nicolet-5DX FTIR spectroscopy device that was equipped with a temperature control mechanism, employing KBr pellets. The measurements were recorded at a constant resolution of 4 cm−1 within the wavenumber spectrum of 4000–500 cm−1. Absorption measurements utilizing X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were conducted at the BL-14 beamline located at the Indus 2 synchrotron radiation source in Indore. XPS was utilized to investigate the stoichiometric composition of the material in its synthesized state. The silicon wafers were utilized as substrates for the deposition of the samples. The stoichiometric ratios of the samples were evaluated based on the relative areas of the peaks corresponding to carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen.

Differential Scanning Calorimeter (DSC) analysis was conducted on a PerkinElmer Pyris 6 DSC instrument at a rate of 10 °C min−1. The glass transition temperature (Tg) was determined by analyzing the thermogram of the second heat cycle, wherein the Tg is indicated by the midpoint value of the heat increment. A Mettler-Toledo TG850 instrument was utilized to conduct Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) within a N2 atmosphere (flow rate = 50 mL min−1) over a temperature range of 25–800 ± 1 °C (heat rate = 51 °C min−1).

Small amplitude oscillatory shear experiments were conducted to investigate the rheology. A fixed stress of 1.0 Pa was applied using an AR 500 rheometer (TA Instruments, Surrey, England). The experimental setup employed the cone-plate geometry with a 2° angle, and a solvent trap consisting of wet sponges and silicone oil was utilized for the measurements. The investigation of the storage and loss modulus was conducted at ambient temperature while considering its frequency dependence.

Prior to conducting mechanical measurements, dried hydrogel samples of height ∼5.77 mm and diameter of ∼18.48 mm were subjected to swelling for a duration of 24 h at room temperature in a PBS buffer with a pH of 7.4. Compression tests were conducted using the Tinius Olsen, England universal testing machine (UTM) model H5KS, and the QMAT 5.37 professional software was utilized for data analysis. The hydrogel samples, which had undergone swelling, were delicately blotted with tissue paper to eliminate any surface moisture before conducting the compressive examination. In addition, every specimen was centrally placed between two compression plates measuring 50 mm in diameter and subjected to a 1000 N load until the desired strain was achieved at a rate of 1 mm min−1. All samples were taken in triplicates (n = 3).

The swelling ratio of nanocomposites was measured utilizing the gravimetric method. The hydrogel discs, with a thickness of approximately 2 mm and a diameter of approximately 6 mm, were weighed (Wi) and subsequently immersed in a 1 × PBS solution at room temperature with a pH of 7.4. The maintenance of constant ionic strength in the buffer solution was achieved through the introduction of a 1 M KCl solution. At predetermined time intervals, the hydrogels were weighed at predetermined time intervals. The surface water was delicately absorbed, and the weight of the swollen hydrogel (Wf) was recorded until it reached equilibrium weight. The equilibrium swelling ratio (ESR) was determined at a specific time point, using the following formula:
ESR
%
=
Wf − Wi
× 100
Wi
Wi refers to the initial dry weight of hydrogels.
Wf represents the final weight of the hydrogels after swelling.

Prior to commencing the experiments, the Institutional Committee for Stem Cell Research (ICSCR/114/20(o)) granted ethical clearance. The human Wharton Jelly derived Mesenchymal Stem Cells (hWJ-MSCs) that had been cryopreserved were revived and subjected to cultivation in a medium consisting of Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium-Low Glucose (Gibco, USA) that was supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (Gibco, USA), along with 1 % Glutamax, 1 % Penicillin-Streptomycin (Gibco, USA), and Amphotericin B (Gibco, USA). The cells were cultured in 25-cm2 flasks at a temperature of 37 °C and were kept in a humidified incubator with 5 % CO2. The culture medium was replaced every other day. The present study utilized cells that were of 3rd to 6th passage.

The hydrogel matrices underwent autoclaving and subsequent soaking in 1 × PBS (pH 7.4) prior to cell seeding. Following the attainment of equilibrium swelling, the hydrogels underwent a process of re-sterilization utilizing UV irradiation. A total of 1 × 105 hWJ-MSCs, resuspended in 10 μL of media, were seeded onto the hydrogel and maintained at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for a period of 3 h to facilitate cell attachment. Following a 3 h incubation period, the cells were treated with DMEM-LG supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % antibiotics. For cell attachment and morphology attainment, cells seeded over nanocomposites were observed for 24 h, post 24 h cells were imaged under Olympus IX-83 (Japan). For SEM analysis, the hydrogels were fixed in Karnovsky's fixative for 1 h at room temperature. Specimens were rinsed twice with PBS and were dehydrated in a gradient manner. Briefly, the gels were gradually dehydrated by an increasing concentration series of ethanol (30 %, 50 %, 70 %, and 90 %) for 20 min each and finally twice in 100 %. The fixed samples were sputter coated with gold and were then observed under SEM [31]. The MTT assay was conducted to assess cell proliferation. The hydrogels underwent initial sterilization and were subsequently allowed to swell overnight in DMEM (low glucose) that was supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % antibiotics. Subsequently, the inflated hydrogels were perforated and arranged in a 96-well plate. A total of 5 × 103 hWJ MSCs cells were introduced onto the swollen gels, followed by the addition of 200 μL of media per well. Subsequently, the cells were placed in a CO2 incubator and maintained at a temperature of 37 °C. The hydrogels were examined using a microscope to assess the extent of cellular adhesion. The MTT assay was conducted on five different occasions, specifically on Day 1, 3, 5, and 9. The plate was subsequently analyzed at a wavelength of 570 nm. The control in the experiment was represented by the utilization of a tissue culture plate (TCP).

A scratch assay was conducted to investigate the migratory characteristics of hWJ-MSCs that were subjected to treatment with CNC-0.1 spent media. The CNC spent media was obtained through the immersion of 1 g of sterile CNC-0.1 in DMEM-LG media containing 10 % FBS and 1 % antibiotics for a duration of 24 h, the utilized media was gathered for subsequent experimentation. hWJ-MSCs were cultured on a 12-well plate and maintained at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 environment until the cells reached a confluency of 70–80 %. Subsequently, the cells were subjected to a 48 h incubation period in a medium devoid of serum. Following incubation, the cellular population reached a state of 80–90 % confluency. Subsequently, PBS was utilized to cleanse the cells, and then a 200 μL pipette tip was employed to scratch the cells. After making the scratch, the cells were subjected to a 1 × PBS wash. Following this, the control well received cell culture medium, while the sample well was treated with CNC-0.1 spent media. The cellular specimens were captured using a 4× magnification at four distinct time points: 0, 24, 48, and 72 h.

The study was granted approval by the Institutional Animal Ethics Committee, in adherence to the guidelines set forth by the Committee for the Purpose of Control and Supervision of Experiments on Animals (CPCSEA) located in New Delhi, India. The synthesis of these nanocomposite hydrogels was conducted with the objective of utilizing them for the purpose of cartilage regeneration. The nanocomposites have the potential to serve as a scaffold for the purpose of filling the voids resulting from chondrocyte mortality or cartilage impairment. Following the transplantation procedure, these scaffolds will come into direct touch with the circulating blood. In order to establish the viability of these nanocomposites in terms of cellular safety, hemocompatibility experiments were conducted. The experiment utilized Wistar albino rats of both genders, which were housed in the Institutional Animal House at Amity University Uttar Pradesh. The rats had an average weight of 280–300 g. The rats received unrestricted access to food and water and were also subjected to consistent diurnal patterns. The blood compatibility of nanocomposites was investigated through an in vitro hemolysis assay. A healthy rat was subjected to retro-orbital blood collection using disposable syringes containing 4.9 % citrate phosphate dextrose adenine solution. The positive control utilized in the study was Triton X, while the negative control was established as Normal saline. In this study, 200 μL of freshly isolated uncoagulated blood was collected in each sample tube and subsequently incubated with different nanocomposites for 1 h under controlled conditions of 5 % CO2, 37 °C temperature, and 95 % humidity. The measurement of absorbance was conducted at a wavelength of 450 nm, and the calculation of the hemolytic rate percentage was performed using the subsequent formula:
Hemolysis
%
=
Sample absorbance − Negative control absorbance
× 100
Positive control absorbance − Negative control absorbance

The differentiation of hWJ-MSCs was achieved through the utilization of the Stempro Chondrogenesis Differentiation Kit, which is a commercially available product (A1007101-Gibco, USA). The nanocomposite matrices were subjected to autoclaving prior to cell seeding, followed by a 48 h soaking period in 1 × PBS (pH 7.2) to ensure complete swelling. Following the swelling process, the hydrogels underwent a subsequent sterilization procedure utilizing UV radiation for a duration of 1 h. In summary, the experiment involved seeding multiple droplets of 10 μL each containing 1 × 105 hWJ-MSCs that were resuspended in chondrocyte differentiation media, onto the hydrogel. A control group was also included, which consisted of TCP in a 35 mm petri dish. Following cell seeding, the petri dish was placed in an incubator set to a temperature of 37 °C and a CO2 concentration of 5 % for a period of 1–2 h to facilitate cell attachment. Following cell adhesion, 2 mL of chondrogenic differentiation media was introduced and the petri dish was maintained in an incubator at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. The media was replaced at intervals of 2–3 days over a period of 18 days, as reported in reference [32]. Upon the conclusion of the 18-day period, the media was removed and the specimens were subjected to visual inspection, resulting in the acquisition of gross images.

In order to perform alcian blue staining, the chondrocyte nodules that were cultivated on tissue culture plates and those grown on nanocomposites were manually removed (after completion of 14 days incubation period). The aforementioned action was undertaken in order to prevent any potential background staining that may have arisen from the absorption of alcian blue stain by the hydrogels, which is known for its notably porous characteristics. Subsequent to the incubation of the detached chondrocytes with 3 % acetic acid for 10 min for cell fixation, they were rinsed with 1 × PBS. The cells were treated according to the laboratory protocol and subsequently subjected to staining with alcian blue (A3157, Sigma, United States) [13]. In brief, a solution of alcian blue was prepared using 3 % acetic acid and subsequently utilized to incubate the samples for a duration of 30 min. After incubation, the pellets containing chondrocytes were subjected to a washing step using 1 × PBS and subsequently air dried. The specimens were then observed under a microscope for imaging purposes.

Following the conclusion of 14 days induction cycle, the cell-NC-0.1 constructs were collected for the purpose of conducting histological analysis. The cells were treated with 4 % formalin for fixation, followed by dehydration using a series of alcohol. Subsequently, the cells were subjected to a solution of xylene I, which was prepared by mixing 100 % ethanol and pure xylene in a 1:1 ratio, for a duration of 30 min. This was followed by soaking the cells in xylene II, which was prepared using pure xylene from Fisher Scientific, for an additional 30 min. Subsequently, the specimens were embedded in paraffin and sectioned into thin slices measuring 5 μm using a microtome. The tissue sections underwent deparaffinization and rehydration procedures, followed by staining with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and Masson's trichrome (MT).

To isolate total RNA from the various groups, the cells were gently removed from the HNS surfaces. The collected cells were rinsed with 1 × PBS and thereafter transferred to a microcentrifuge tube. The chondrocyte nodules were disrupted using TRI reagent (Molecular Research Centre, Ohio, USA) and the total RNA was extracted using the phenol chloroform technique. The Nanophotometer (Implen, Germany) was used to measure the optical density and concentration of the samples. The cDNA of both control and HNS chondrocytes was produced using 1 μg/μL of RNA samples, with the assistance of the reverse transcriptase (RT) enzyme (786-50919, Gbiosciences, India). The housekeeping gene GAPDH was utilized to normalize the generated cDNA samples. The qRT-PCR analysis was conducted using the SYBR green Master Mix (Kapa, KK4601, Sigma-Aldrich, United States) according to the manufacturer's instructions, utilizing the CFX96 instrument (Bio-Rad, United States). The list of primers associated to chondrocytes may be found in Table 2 . Additionally, all samples were collected in triplicate (n = 3).

The standard error of the mean was used to express the data. One-way analysis of variance and post-hoc Dunnett's test were used in the statistical study. The Student t-test was used to compare the two groups. GraphPad prism program was used for all statistical analysis. A “p” value of 0.05 or less was regarded as statistically significant. The findings of each experiment were presented as the mean standard deviation, and each experiment was carried out in triplicate.

XRD is a fundamental analytical technique used to ascertain the crystal structure, degree of crystallinity, and interplanar spacing of crystalline materials. The degree of crystallinity (D) can have a significant impact on the material's properties, such as its transparency and diffusion characteristics. Fig. 1a shows the XRD spectra of nanocomposite CNC-0.05 and CNC-0.1 have been shown, wherein when CQDs of different concentration are incorporated into the polymer matrix to form a nanocomposite, the XRD patterns depict a combination of broad peaks. CNC-0.05 exhibited some amorphous nature, with a cyclohexane monoclinic carbon phase at 32.5° having (2′11) planes (JCPDS file 48- 1960) and a cubic phase at 34.5° having (210) planes (JCPDS file 18-0311) The presence of the higher amount of CQDs can lead to a decrease in the intensity of the sharp peaks associated with the polymer matrix, signifying a decrease in the degree of crystallinity, which is confirmed by the Scherer's equation (Table 3 ), wherein, (K = 0.89),(λ = 1.54 A°), β = full width half maxima value of the material related to the crystallographic plane. Here in, CQDs can act as nucleation sites, disrupting the formation of ordered crystalline regions in the polymer matrix, D value is found to be decrease from 0.69 to 0.52, which is in good agreement with decrease in crystallinity of the nanocomposite with the increase in nanofiller concentration.

Numerous investigations have documented of the hydrogel crystallinity can facilitate differentiation of stem cells into multiple lineages [33,34]. During the advanced phase of cartilage development, chondrocytes manufacture collagen as a key component of the extracellular matrix, which aligns with high collagen content observed in our MT staining (Fig. 8e). Previously, chondrocytes have been reported to undergo transformation from a spherical to a flat shape as the crystallinity increased. These findings suggest a significant correlation between the crystallinity of hydrogels and the endochondral ossification of chondrocytes [35]. Several reports indicate that hydrogel crystallinity may facilitate the chondrogenic differentiation of stem cells by providing a conducive microenvironment for cellular adhesion, proliferation, and differentiation [33]. The degree of crystallinity may be contingent upon the distinct properties of the hydrogel and the type of cell that is being utilized. The enhanced crystalline structure of the scaffolds potentially influences the process of chondrocyte proliferation and migration. Additionally, scaffolds with elevated crystallinity increase the elastic modulus and elongation at the point of rupture. Alterations in the crystalline nature of the nanocomposite hydrogel results in indirect modification in its architecture highlighting the correlation between hydrogel crystallinity and chondrocyte differentiation [36]. Multiple research endeavors have corroborated the pivotal function of matrix stiffness, encompassing both native ECM and artificially created substrates, in influencing cellular choices and tissue growth through the implementation of tissue engineering methodologies. Interestingly, ECM serves as the primary housing environment for cells, facilitating the transmission of both external mechanical loads and internal mechanical stimulation to resident cells. Through the modulation of cell growth and differentiation, ECM plays a crucial role in guiding the fate of these cells. In the interim, cells can modify the composition and structure of the macromolecular network by releasing matrix components and matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) or applying mechanical forces to control the type and organization of macromolecules in the ECM. This process contributes to the diverse range of tissue stiffness observed, which varies from the pliable brain to the rigid cortical bone.

SEM micrographs of the swollen hybrid hydrogels are shown in Fig. 1. In the absence of CQDs (NH), the delaminated areas of the polymer sheets experience deformation when subjected to uniaxial forces, resulting in the formation of parallel ridges on the surface and the emergence of a stripped architecture, as depicted in Fig. 1b. The higher the amount of water intact in between the polymeric network, greater will be the size of pores formed. The introduction of 0.01 % CQDs results in the deformation of delaminated buckles along the ridge due to interfacial energy, leading to a partial collapse of the structure. A novel buckle emerges at a right angle to the pre-existing one, resulting in a complex labyrinthine structure, as illustrated in Fig. 1c. The interconnected honeycomb structure of the CNCs following increasing CQDs concentration is highlighted in Fig. 1(d-f). We believe that the high surface to volume ratio and uniformly distributed pores obtained with anisotropic growth mode serve to confirm the structural effectiveness. The presence of basic (amide) and acidic (carboxylic) functional groups, of the monomeric unit along with the presence of CQDs, undergo protonation and deprotonation in response to pH, is also credited with the creation of internal crosslinked networks in this macroporous structure. The interaction of CQDs (0.1 %) with DMAEMA, enabled its exfoliation (Fig. 1g) [37]. The sequential addition of acrylic acid laid the foundation for electrolytic complexation with DMAEMA resulting in the formation of polymer matrix after the addition of initiator. The interaction of CQDs and polymeric chain altered the interfacial tension between the polymeric complex and water, resulting in the formation of aggregates within the polymer matrix. The presence of substantial pores within hydrogels has been observed to exert a significant influence on the process of chondrocyte differentiation. Study by Motta et al. 2010, highlighted the role of increased pore size and porosity, promote efficient nutrient mass transfer and metabolic waste removal. This may lead to an increase in cell proliferation and a more uniform distribution of cells [38,39]. Hydrogels have gained significant attraction in the field of tissue engineering due to their ability to emulate the physical and mechanical attributes of native tissues, such as cartilage. The manipulation of pore size and its distribution is a determining factor in regulating the porosity of hydrogels, ultimately influencing cellular responses and tissue development [37,40–42].

Additionally, the mechanical rigidity of the hydrogel can also influence the differentiation of stem cells into chondrocytes. It can be facilitated by the existence of growth factors or extracellular matrix constituents. Additionally, the mechanical rigidity of the hydrogel can have an impact on cellular activity and tissue development. In tissue engineering applications, the promotion of chondrocyte differentiation and functional cartilage tissue formation can be significantly influenced by the porosity and other properties of hydrogels [43,44]. SAED pattern of the nanocomposite at higher CQD concentration (0.1 %), demonstrates a ring pattern which is in stark contrast with the highly crystalline orientation of the nanocomposite at lower concentration of CQDs (0.05 %) (Fig. 1h and i). The alternate bright spots at different hkl plane in the SAED pattern, possibly corresponds to the intrinsic lattice misalignment prompting the formation of packing motifs. At higher CQDs concentration, the SAED pattern of the nanocomposite portrays the crystalline nature of CQDs with well-maintained integrity and uniformity of lattice structure.

XPS measurements were conducted to examine the composition and quantitative analysis of the nanocomposite in its as-synthesized state. The XPS survey spectrum given in Fig. 2 demonstrates that both the CNCs contain carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen. When 0.05 % (w/v) CQDs are present in CNC-0.05 functional groups including O−H, −COOH, and −SO3H, undergoes deprotonation and interact with the N+ (tertiary amine) of DMAEMA, forming an electrolytic complex. This is supported by the presence of a prominent peak at 402.8 eV (C−N+) and 404.7 eV (SO3−N+), in the XPS spectra indicating the formation of these complexes. In the deconvoluted N 1s spectra of CNC-0.05 (Fig. 2av-viii), the addition of APS and TEMED lead to the formation of free radicals of AAc and DMAEMA. DMAEMA radicals are more stabilized by the inductive effect of the CH3 group, facilitating their reaction with CQDs compared to AAc. This is supported by the higher binding energy shift of the C−C/C−H component in the deconvoluted C 1s spectra of CNC-0.05 (287.5 eV) (Fig. 2a) compared to NH (285 eV) [45]. The presence of a peak at 402.8 eV in the deconvoluted N 1s spectra of CNC-0.05 indicates the effective grafting of PDMAEMA chains onto the surface of CQDs (Fig. 2avii). Furthermore, the deconvolution of the O1s peak reveals two components at 533.19 eV and 534.54 eV, corresponding to CO and C=O/O-C=O, respectively. However, when we increase the filler concentration 0.1 % (CNC-0.1) resulting spectra exhibit different traits. The C 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks, centered at 286.14 eV for CO, other at 288.09 eV showing the presence of C-N/C-O. The O 1s spectra of CNC-0.1 (Fig. 2aiv) comprises two peaks located at 533.54 and 534.59 eV, confirming that oxygen occurs in two distinct forms, C=O/O-C=O and CO. The highest CO intensity among all O 1s peaks shows that the CO group is more exposed at the edge sites of CNC-0.1 rather than at the basal plane sites, resulting in a defect-free structure, as reported by other groups. The N 1s spectra of CNC-0.1 in (Fig. 2aiii) supported the emergence of the C-N+ bonding.

While working with nanocomposites, several questions arose in our mind. Firstly, what role CQD concentration plays in making the dawn of carbon nanocomposite (CNC) structures? What function does the polymer matrix perform in activating their emergence, and what is the underlying mechanism in that process? To find out the answers of these concerns, we first inspect the chemical features of two different CNCs having lower and higher concentration of CQD named as CNC-0.1 and CNC-0.05. A notable finding for CNC-0.05 was that its FTIR spectra illustrated large -OH stretching bands at 3330 cm−1, (Fig. 2b). Furthermore, the peak at 2970 cm−1 in CNC-0.05 showing the CH stretching which in accordance with the XPS C 1s spectra of CNC-0.05 (287.5 eV). The peaks at about 1575 cm−1 and 1720 cm−1 indicate the presence of CO group of amides and -C=O group of ketone, respectively. The peaks located at 1165 and 1257 cm−1 show the presence of C-OH and C-O-C stretching group in the CNC-0.05. These findings suggest that CQDs feature large number of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surfaces, which are prone to integrating with chemical compounds. In the case of CNC-0.1 with the increasing concentration of CQDs the intensity of the characteristic peaks associated with the CQDs, such as those related to CC (1637 cm−1) stretching modes, may increase. This is because the increased concentration of CQDs leads to a higher probability of interaction between the CQDs and the incident infrared radiation. For example, the peak associated with the CH stretching mode of the polymer matrix shifted slightly to lower wavenumbers of 2965 cm−1 in our case, indicating a change in the local environment of the polymer chains owing to the occurrence of the CQDs [46]. Furthermore, as the concentration of CQDs increases, there is an increase in the intensity of the broad peak associated with the -OH stretching mode of the polymer matrix, indicating an increase in the amount of intermolecular hydrogen bonding in the system.

Raman spectroscopy, a benign method, was employed to examine the degree of structural disorder in the carbon matrix based on various defect types, including boundary grains, point imperfections, edges, doping, vacancies, functional groups, etc. Herein, Raman spectra of the CNC-0.1 and CNC-0.05 has been depicted in Fig. 2c. In CNC-0.1, distinctive conjugated peaks at 2940 and 2980 cm−1 represent symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of CH3 groups [47]. The N 1s spectra of CNC-0.05 was deconvoluted and a peak at 402.8 eV were observed, which corresponds to the C-N+ bond, indicates that poly-DMAEMA chains were effectively grafted on the surface of CQDs. The presence of alkyl chains grafted on CQDs was also confirmed by Raman spectroscopy, where a sharp peak at ∼1310 cm−1 was observed in the CNC-0.05 sample (Fig. 2c). The sharp and robust Raman bands at ∼2946 and ∼2970 cm−1 in CNC-0.05 showing symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration respectively. The Raman peak at 2830 cm−1 in CNC-0.05 is associated with the symmetric stretching vibration of CH2 groups which were masked in the CNC-0.1.

The thermal stability of CNC-0.05 and CNC-0.1 were investigated by TGA. TGA thermograms of CNC-0.05 and CNC-0.1 decipher a four-stage thermal degradation process (Fig. 3a-c). The first stage degradation starts at ∼46 °C and ranges to ∼170 °C with an apparent high mass loss of 3 % to 18 % in case of CNC-0.05, whereas slightly lower mass loss of 2.5 % to 10 % in case of CNC-0.1. This can be ascribed to the loss of adsorbed water from the polymer matrices. The second stage thermal breakdown initiates at ∼240 °C with a higher weight loss of 37.5 % in CNC-0.05 compared to CNC-0.1 with a lower weight loss of ∼33 %, corresponding to the high thermal stability of the CNC-0.1. The slow rate of degradation observed initially in two stages of CNC-0.1 suggests that there is a strong interfacial interaction between the polymer-filler complexes [48]. This strong interaction attributed to the uniform intercalation of the filler material within the polymer matrices. The third stage of degradation varies in all the cases with an earlier degradation in CNC-0.05 at ∼335 °C with an apparent mass loss of 51 %.The final stage of degradation at ∼442 °C was accompanied by ∼95.5 % weight loss in CNC-0.05 whereas it was comparatively lesser in CNC-0.1 (95 %), corresponding to the breakdown of polymer backbone which is slightly lower in the CNC-0.1. The higher concentration nanofiller in CNC-0.1 correlates with overall slower rate of decomposition and higher elasticity and flexibility of the nanocomposite, which is lies in good agreement with the results of the rheological analysis.

Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) is an efficient way to elucidate the CNC heat of fusion, glass transition (Tg), temperature of crystallization (Tc), exothermic and endothermic behavior. The concentration of CQDs in a polymer nanocomposite stimulus the thermal behavior of the material as observed in the DSC curve. The DSC heating and cooling curves for both nanocomposites as presented in Fig. 3d, in the case of a CQD based polymer matrix, the DSC plot would typically show the Tg, melting temperature (Tm), and other thermal events such as crystallization or degradation. As the temperature increases, the CNC-0.1 undergoes a glass transition, which is reflected as an endothermic peak, while for CNC-0.05 it is showing an exothermic peak. In addition, the presence of CQDs also affects the Tg and Tm of the composite. Ordinarily, an increase in CQD concentration cause to a shift in Tg due to the increased rigidity of the polymer matrix which is reflect in CNC-0.1, which correspond to the interaction between the CQDs and polymer matrix and depicts a perfection in the thermal stability of the carbon nanocomposite. The presence of CQDs also affects the degree of melting, which is shown in the DSC curve of CNC-0.1 and CNC-0.05, as changes in the peak area or height. The current work has investigated stem cell differentiation by CNC which is directly related to the elasticity and surface mobile nature of CNC [49]. Mobility is related to the Tg of CNC, here Tg for CNC-0.1 is ∼50 °C lower than CNC-0.05, which is attributed to surface mobile nature of CNC-0.1.This property lies in good agreement with the lower elastic modulus value (217.4 KPa) of CNC-0.1 which responsible for stem cell differentiation. The correlation between the hydrogel's glass transition and the induction of chondrocyte from stem cells is a multifaceted phenomenon that is contingent upon several variables. The hydrogel's glass transition temperature (Tg) denotes the temperature at which the substance undergoes a transition from a state of higher rigidity, characterized by a glassy texture, to a state of lower rigidity, characterized by a rubbery texture. The hydrogel's mechanical properties and flexibility are impacted [50–52]. A hydrogel possessing a Tg that is appropriate can furnish an ideal mechanical milieu for the differentiation of chondrocytes. The material exhibits potential to replicate the inherent rigidity of endogenous cartilage, thereby facilitating appropriate cellular adhesion, expansion, and specialization. Hydrogels that possess a lower glass transition temperature tend to demonstrate elevated flexibility and comparatively softer mechanical characteristics, which bear resemblance to the inherent milieu of cartilage tissue. The utilization of this substrate has the potential to facilitate a more optimal environment for the differentiation and maturation of chondrocytes derived from stem cells. The hydrogel's mechanical characteristics, such as its elasticity and stiffness, have the potential to impact cellular activities such as adhesion, proliferation, and differentiation. Through the replication of the physical attributes inherent in the cartilage matrix, hydrogels possessing a reduced glass transition temperature have the potential to amplify the chondrogenic differentiation of stem cells, thereby stimulating the generation of operational chondrocytes. It is imperative to acknowledge that the process of chondrocyte induction is intricate and influenced by various factors such as growth factors, signaling molecules, and cell-cell interactions. The correlation between the glass transition of a hydrogel and chondrocyte induction is a complex matter that necessitates meticulous examination of the distinct hydrogel characteristics, stem cell variety, and cultivation circumstances.

Physiological functions, such as bearing physiological stresses and protecting tissues from rupture, rely on substrate stiffness at the macroscopic scale. Load-bearing tissues provide a special purpose owing to macroscopic mechanical anisotropy. Walking and running, for example, need less total energy because the tendon's aligned fibrous architecture of ECM can withstand tensile stresses by permitting energy storage and dissipation to convey force along the tendon. The scaffolds have been augmented with nanoparticles to enhance their mechanical properties. Each distinct tissue possesses unique characteristics that must be considered when designing a scaffold. The fabricated scaffolds and living cells exhibit mutual mechanical properties. Moreover, from an alternative perspective, it is imperative that the scaffolds exhibit sufficient mechanical characteristics while undergoing in vitro cultivation in bioreactors, especially when subjected to mechanical stimuli, to prevent the disintegration of the tissue-engineered construct [37,53,54].

Usually, hydrogels have poor mechanical properties, but it was amazing to see addition of CQDs as a nanofiller enhanced the properties of hydrogels. The introduction of various concentration-based CQD nanofillers serves as a stimulant and provides charge-charge repulsion between the individual filler particles, enhanced stress disintegration, and in certain circumstances additional stabilization particularly towards compression which is manifested in both CNC-0.1 and CNC-0.05 [48]. Rheological experiments were performed to investigate the mechanical strength and viscoelastic response of the synthesized CNC typically showing the existence of highly interconnected nanostructure with anisometric nanofillers (0.1 wt% and 0.05 wt%) in the nanocomposite system (Fig. 4a). The oscillatory frequency and temperature sweep measurements were taken at different concentrations of CQD in the CNC to optimize the distribution of nanofillers within the polymer network. The higher value of storage modulus (G′) (27,290 Pa) in CNC-0.05 at constant angular frequency in contrast to CNC-0.1 (18,405 Pa) is in good agreement with the swelling result. G′ value declines with increase in temperature in both case which represent the non-chemical/covalent cross-linking. Loss factor (Tan δ ) in CNC-0.1(0.05384) at angular frequency of 200 rad sec −1 signifies viscoelasticity impact generated due to addition of nanofiller during the phase separation of the polymerization process. CNCs particularly CNC-0.1 becomes much more extensive than CNC-0.05 owing to their combined effect of covalent polymer cross-linking with polymer-nanofiller interactions inducing higher degree of viscoelasticity.

The Mechanical property of CNC has been calculated for determining the compressive strength and young's modulus value for both the nanocomposite (Fig. 4b and Table 4 ). The mechanical profile of CNC-0.05 exhibit remarkable competency in comparison to CNC-0.1, revealing significant increases in the young's modulus (690.74 kPa) and compressive strength value (445.53 kPa), while lower value of young's modulus (217.4 kPa) and compressive strength (119.6 kPa) in CNC-0.1 demonstrating that nanocomposite is going towards softer nature in case of CNC-0.1 in contrast to CNC-0.05, this lower value of young modulus of CNC-0.1 match with our swelling ratio values of CNC which is higher in CNC-0.1 and lower in CNC-0.05 [55], which aids in stem cell differentiation. The increase in the softer nature of CNC-0.1 (217.4 kPa) is ascribed to the rearrangement of physical interactions between polymer matrix and nanofiller within the covalently cross-linked networks letting higher elongations.

Surface stiffness plays a very important role in cell attachment and proliferation. Studies have demonstrated the variations between 2D and 3D settings in the response of cell spreading and proliferation to substrate stiffness [56]. We discovered that hWJ-MSCs proliferation on the surface of the gels was stiffness-dependent. Similar proliferation activity was seen with three distinct fibroblast types on gel surface, which contrasted sharply with that in 3D gels. The increase in hydrogel stiffness prevented the cell from spreading and constrained its proliferation [57]. The reported findings align with our cytocompatibility results. CNC-0.1 has lower young's modulus of ∼217kPA but has higher proliferation rate when compared to CNC-0.05.

In a study conducted by Yang et al. 2010, greater chondrogenic differentiation was produced by soft matrix, whereas stiff matrix had the reverse effect and likely inhibited chondrogenic differentiation due to reduced mass transfer [58]. The flexible hydrogel has the potential to serve as a biomimetic scaffold for cartilage regeneration by giving hWJ-MSCs a favorable environment for chondrogenic development. To encourage the distinct development of stem cells into chondrocytes, a softer gel's function is to replicate the physical characteristics of the natural cartilage tissue. A more physiologically appropriate mechanical environment resembling cartilage is created when stem cells are cultivated in a softer gel, such as a hydrogel with a reduced stiffness. We believe that mechanical stimulus affects the destiny and behavior of stem cells, and their differentiation to a particular lineage [59,60]. Plausibly, softer gel can participate in activation of certain signaling pathways, such as the transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-β) pathway, that are important in chondrogenic development. Additionally, the mechanical characteristics of the gel can control the chondrocyte-related extracellular matrix synthesis and gene expression patterns [61]. The softer gel can also help stem cells grow and organize in three dimensions, enabling them to create structures that resemble cartilage and encouraging the deposition of an extracellular matrix that resembles natural cartilage tissue. In conclusion, a softer gel creates a mechanical microenvironment that directs stem cell differentiation towards chondrocytes, simulating the characteristics of cartilage tissue and encouraging the growth of functional cartilage-like structures.

UV-visible spectra of (a) CNC-0.05 and (b) CNC-0.1 display an strong absorption band at λmax ∼ 225 nm and 234 that ascribed to the π–π* transition of CC bond in both the CNC-0.05 and CNC-0.1 material, while the weak shoulder at ∼270–400 nm was recognized for n−π* transition of CO bond in both CNCs (Fig. 5 a-b) [62]. To quantitatively compare the results, the UV–Vis data was subjected to the direct band-gap (Eg) calculation in line with established literature reports. The band gap is determined using the formula (ahν)n = β(hν-Eg) where β = constant, Eg is material's band gap, exponent n depends on the type of transition, and a = absorption coefficient that derived by using the formula a = 2.303 A/t. From the calculation Eg value was found to be 4.08 for CNC-0.1 and 4.24 for CNC-0.05, decrement in the Eg value from (4.24–4.08) with increase in the loading wt% of CQD were witnessed. The band gap reduction in the case of CNC-0.1 (4.08 eV) can be attributed to the introduction of additional energy states within the band gap, which decreases the energy needed to stimulate electrons from the valence band to the conduction band. The lower Eg value obtained in the current CNC-0.1 system demonstrates its potential for a variety of electro-optical applications [63].

The optical characteristics of CNC have been studied by employing the steady state fluorescence spectroscopy technique. The obtained CNC exhibited a wide spectrum and emitted light with a visible range of wavelengths that attained the near infrared (Fig. 5c-d). This rendered them more efficient as a light-capturing medium for nanophotonic uses like bioimaging. The PL spectra of both CNC-0.1 and CNC-0.05 exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra, and the intensity of CNC decreases with increase of excitation wavelength in both cases, which can be attributed to several distinctive characteristics, including the impact of quantum confinement, change in elemental structure, size effects and edge positions [64]. This phenomenon can lead to the formation of diverse emissive trap sites and electron density on the exterior of CQDs. The excitation of CNC at lower and higher wavelength causes a structured emission with two emission maxima respectively. Interestingly, excitation at 330 and 550 nm exhibit emission at 344 and 578 nm, for CNC-0.1 while for CNC-0.05 at 330 and 550 nm it shows emission band at 345 and 580 nm respectively, as shown in Fig. 5(a and b), calculated value of Stokes shift increased from 14 nm to 28 nm, in the case of CNC-0.1,similarly for CNC-0.05 it exhibit small increase in stoke shift value from 15 to 30 nm, with the change in excitation wavelength from 330 nm to 500. In the CNC-0.1 lower value of stock shift is due to presence of more quantum confinement effect resulting in larger separation between excitation and emission energy. This change in stokes shift and small emission band shift in CNC luminescence spectra lets in optimal coupling with additional fluorophores or quantum dots with the produced light [65]. The results of PL spectra in accordance with the findings of FTIR and XPS investigation which depicts the existence of oxygen containing functional group on surface of CNC-0.1 and CNC-0.05 [66].

When immersed in PBS for a given time and weighed, we determined the enlargement percentage of the pure and nanocomposite samples. There is a direct correlation between CQDs concentration and nanocomposite expansion, as shown in Fig. 6a. Swelling efficiency of the CQD-Hybrid gels was directly proportional to the CQDs concentration, as we increased the CQDs concentration in the hydrogel, the swelling ratio of the nanocomposite increased. After 120 h, the respective equilibrium swelling ratios (ESR) of NH, CNC-0.01, CNC-0.05, CNC-0.1 and CNC-0.3 were 990 %, 1200 %, 1300 %, 1400 % and 2400 %, respectively (Fig. 6d). The nanocomposite hydrogel with the highest concentration of CQDs (0.3 %) has a greater swelling percentage. The identification of disparities in CQDs-hybrid gels can be expedited and simplified through the utilization of color and swelling as discerning factors. Scheme 1 and Fig. 6b depict the reflectance spectra of the digital (visual) photographs. The utilization of digital photography has facilitated the observation of discernible alterations in the color of nanocomposite hydrogels. The incorporation of CQDs into the synthesis procedure resulted in an anticipated alteration in hue from achromatic to a tinge of yellow brown. The data presented in Fig. 6a indicates that during the initial 8 h, the hydrogels exhibited a swift increase in volume, which subsequently approached a state of equilibrium. The primary cause of the initial swelling phenomenon is attributed to the hydrogen bonding interaction that occurs between the functional groups of water molecules and the polymer. Furthermore, a structure resembling a network was formed because of the hydrogen bonding that occurred between the water molecules that were bound and additional water molecules. The increased swelling of the hydrogel is attributed to the elevated concentration of CQDs that permeate the network. These results are in good agreement with the already reported literature [67–69]. One of the most significant obstacles that conventional scaffold faces are their inability to provide a platform for intercellular communication [70]. Plausibly the CNC enables cell contact, resulting in increased cell proliferation, growth, and colonization [71–73].

We studied the ability of the produced hydrogel scaffolds to promote hWJ-MSC adhesion and proliferation to determine if they could be appropriate for tissue engineering objectives. Each nanocomposite hydrogel created in this study was assessed to evaluate its cytocompatibility. Our group is working in this arena, and we have developed several biocompatible nanocomposite hydrogels [13,29]. CQDs are bestowed with several superior properties, such as excellent biocompatibility, solubility, and chemical inertness. As CQDs are increasing the swelling efficiency of the synthesized gels, it is believed that CQDs will improve the nanocomposites biocompatibility. In this experiment, we examined how nanofillers (CQDs) affected the biological interactions between cells and hybrid hydrogels. In Fig. 7a, we compared the cell attachment efficiency of CNC's post 24 h incubation, Fig. 7c shows the SEM image of CNC-0.1 (24 h post cell seeding). The attachment of hWJ-MSCs over CNCs was similar in all the groups except CNC-0.3. CNC-0.3 had an ESR of 2400 % (Fig. 6d), which made it highly porous and unmanageable. Though the cells have attached over these gels and have attained proper morphologies, but the cells are far apart as compared to the other groups. This gap between hWJ-MSCs could be attributed to the high swelling property of these CNCs gels. Once cell attachment was confirmed, we wanted to see if these hybrids are having any effect on cell proliferation or not. Rheological investigations predict that the optimal displacement of an embedded object, such as a cell, in a hydrogel is associated with softer scaffolds [74,75]. The present study reveals that the CNC-hydrogel facilitates the proliferation of hWJ-MSCs (Fig. 7b). For this study MTT assay was performed for 9 days and tissue culture plate (TCP) was used as a control. On day 1, the proliferation rate on CNCs was lower as compared to the TCP. This phenomenon may have occurred because TCP provides the ideal condition for cell attachment and proliferation whereas the cells were taking some time in adapting to the hydrogel environment. On day 3 and 5, the proliferation rate of cells grown over CNCs was quite comparable (except CNC-0.3) with the cells growing over TCP. As compared to TCP, there was a stark increase in cell proliferation of cells grown over CNCs on day 7 and day 9. This might be attributed to the fact that TCP is a form of 2D culture, so once the cells attained full confluency, contact inhibition was observed [76,77]. Incorporating CQDs within the hydrogel matrix serves a dual purpose. The nanoparticles function as a cross-linking agent, inducing softening of the hydrogel without compromising its structural integrity, thereby facilitating the infiltration of cells into the compact matrix. Secondly, it is also hypothesized that CQDs-hydrogels facilitate cellular adhesion and viability, thereby serving as a viable substrate for cellular proliferation. Furthermore, the significant porosity of the scaffolds facilitated cellular migration while preserving their viability. When used for culture, CNC-0.3 absorbed the highest volume of media and had very viscous consistency, owing to this cell were far apart in this group and its cytocompatibility was also depleted. CNC-0.1 had highest proliferation rate therefore it was used in further experiments. Owing to their cell attachment and cytocompatibility, for material characterization, CNC-0.05 and CNC-0.1 were used in most of the studies.

As already reported in Mukherjee et al. 2023 [29], CQDs helped in cell migration. In order to check whether CQDs were released from the hybrid gels into the media, or if they are intact in the polymer matrix. To check this theory, we performed an indirect migration assay, wherein we soaked CNC-0.1 in DMEM-LG media (supplemented with FBS and antibiotics) for 24 h. Post 24 h, spent media was used for assessing the migration of hWJ-MSCs. As per our observation, there was no significant difference in the migration rate of cells growing in normal media and in CNC-0.1 spent media. These results indicated that either the CQDs were not released into the spent medium or the amount of CQDs released was not sufficient to cause any change in migration potential of the cells (Fig. 7d).

For analysis of hemocompatibility of synthesized hybrid-hydrogels, as mentioned in methodology section, the gels were incubated in freshly extracted wistar rats blood and after incubation, absorbance was noted down (Fig. 7e). We also formed the smear of the RBCs (on glass slides) incubated with the samples and imaged them under bright field microscope (Fig. 7f). Post incubation with CNCs, RBCs were collected and centrifuged to separate cells and serum (Fig. 7g). All the collected data showed that the CNCs were hemocompatible and their results were in line with the normal saline (positive control). Here we have used triton X as negative control and all readings were taken in triplicates.

Materiobiologists have focused on different forms of carbon allotropes to stimulate differentiation in MSCs. While there have been some first attempts in this subject, the significant progress of this strategy has generated enthusiasm among scholars worldwide. Liu et al. conducted a study to investigate the impact of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the ability of mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) to self-renew and differentiate into adipocytes and osteocytes. The aim was to validate the presence of pluripotent stem cells. Their study showcased the absorption of MSCs by GQDs, resulting in the activation of osteogenic and adipogenic genes through the bone morphogenic protein and transforming growth factor-β (TGF-β) signaling pathway, MAPK pathway, extracellular matrix (ECM)-receptor interaction, Wnt signaling pathway, Notch signaling pathway, focal adhesion, and calcium signaling pathway [78]. In addition, Yang's team synthesized carbon dots (CDs) from citric acid using a chemical method to monitor and enhance the process of positive osteogenesis in MSCs. By doing their research, they noted that the ROS-mediated mitogen activated protein kinase (MAPK) pathway helps to promote osteogenesis. Additionally, they found that the inherent optical features of CDs allowed for the visualization of stem cells [79]. Similarly, Shen and colleagues proposed that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) possess osteoinductive properties, which can be utilized for bone defect regeneration. They achieved this by creating a hybrid hydrogel that consisted of negatively charged CQDs (n-CQD) co-encapsulated within a GeIM hydrogel scaffold [80].

Both CQDs and hydrogels (synthesized with DMAEMA and acrylic acid) played crucial role in the differentiation of chondrocytes. In order to advance, we sought to investigate the potential of CNCs, which were prepared utilizing DMAEMA, acrylic acid, and CQDs, in the induction of chondrocytes [13,29]. Initially, hWJ-MSCs were seeded onto the hydrogels in accordance with the pre-existing laboratory protocol. Following cell seeding, the scaffolds (NH and CNC-0.1) underwent an 18 day incubation with periodic media replacement. Following the designated incubation period, visual imaging was conducted on the hydrogels, while the TCP was utilized as a control. While the quantity of chondrocyte nodules cultivated over CNCs was comparatively lower than that of TCP, the dimensions of the former were significantly larger than the latter (Fig. 8 a). Upon comparison of NH and CNC-0.1, it was observed that the latter exhibited a greater quantity and larger dimensions of nodules. During the experiment, it was observed that mature chondrocyte nodules were formed by the conclusion of the 14th day. In our subsequent experimental procedure, we decreased the duration of incubation to 14 days. Upon completion of an experiment, we conducted alcian blue staining, which is a cationic chromophore that exhibits an affinity towards the anionic sulfate and carboxyl functional groups that are present in the proteoglycan macromolecules. The fundamental tenet underlying alcian blue staining is that the dye molecules exhibit an affinity for the proteoglycans present in the extracellular matrix, thereby inducing their visualization under a microscope. The correlation between staining intensity and proteoglycan quantity in a sample renders it as an invaluable tool for investigating the advancement of chondrocyte induction [81–83]. The Alcian blue findings suggest that after a 14-day incubation period, chondrocytes cultivated over TCP exhibited a relatively weaker staining intensity when compared to the other two groups, indicating an incomplete induction (Fig. 8ci). The observed stain uptake by the chondrocyte nodules in both experimental groups exhibited comparable visual characteristics (Fig. 8c ii, iii). However, the CNC-0.1 group displayed a larger size in comparison to the NH group, which suggests the maturation of chondrocyte nodules within a 14-day period of growth in the former group. The process of chondrogenesis is marked by the initial condensation of loosely arranged mesenchymal tissues, which subsequently form cellular aggregates that exhibit active expression of diverse extracellular matrix and cell adhesion molecules. Recent reports have indicated that water molecule confined to a subsurface gradient field, modifies the molecular interactions with surfaces [84–86]. However, the underlying mechanism of this phenomenon remains unclear. The aforementioned alterations, in conjunction with the hydrophobic-hydrophilic equilibrium and amphiphilic gradients, have a considerable impact on protein adsorption. Histological analysis was conducted using H&E staining on the 14th day to confirm the distinct proliferative and differentiation potential of the nodule grown over CNC-0.1. The observation of cell aggregation in the presence of CNC-0.1 revealed the preservation of clear boundaries and a more developed phenotype (Fig. 8d). The extracellular matrix (ECM) exhibited a chondromyxoid-like characteristic, accompanied by pericellular lacunae that bear a striking resemblance to the chondrocytes. The observed cellular structures in CNC-0.1 exhibited an increase in both size and quantity of pericellular lacunae. The MT stain revealed a significant prominence of the blue color, which is a characteristic of collagen (Fig. 8e). This indicates the presence of a substantial amount of collagen in the nodule that has developed over CNC-0.1.

Cartilage tissue is primarily categorized into three zones: the superficial zone, middle zone, and deep zone. The expression of collagen II (col 2) is higher in the superficial zone, whereas the middle zone has a greater expression of aggrecan (ACAN). The deep zone, on the other hand, predominantly comprises collagen X (col 10) [87,88]. col 2 serves as the primary structural component of cartilage, while ACAN acts as the main proteoglycan (GAG). ACAN is formed of chondroitin and keratin sulfate as the core proteins and is an essential part of the extracellular matrix (ECM) in cartilage tissue. To investigate the process of fast chondrogenesis at the gene level, we conducted qRT-PCR analysis on four groups: the uninduced control group, the induced TCP group, the induced NH group, and the induced CNC-0.1 group. The presence of both col 2 and ACAN in the chondrocyte nodules cultivated in all three groups verified the buildup of chondrogenic specific extracellular matrix (ECM) in all groups, suggesting favorable cell-matrix interactions. While ACAN and col 2 are expressed in all three induced groups, the expression of both markers in CNC-0.1 is much greater compared to the other two groups. Previous research has demonstrated that the negatively charged surface promotes the development of cartilage cells by increasing the activity of particular genes such as ACAN, col 2, GAG, and others [89]. Furthermore, the presence of carboxylic acid abundant surfaces and CQDs in CNC-0.1 is responsible for the stimulation of different indicators, thus clarifying the enhanced cell clustering and elevated expression of chondro-specific indicators within a 14-day period. Collagen I (col 1) and II are both important indicators of chondrogenesis. However, when col 2 is overexpressed compared to col 1, it indicates a higher production of matrix and a morphology more similar to chondrocytes [90]. In the CNC-0.1 group, the level of col 1 expression was markedly elevated in comparison to the NH and TCP groups. Col 1 exhibited strong expression in CNC-0.1, however col 1 showed a higher expression in CNC-0.1 compared to collagen I in all sample groups. A higher concentration of col 2, as opposed to col 1, indicates a superior level of preservation of the original chondrocyte. The expression of collagen X (col 10) was significantly higher in the CNC-0.1 group compared to the other groups, indicating that this group maintained the cartilage lineage [91].

Cartilage is a connective tissue that has a distinctive nature and a restricted ability to regenerate itself. The avascular nature of cartilage poses a challenge for efficient healing as it lacks a direct blood supply, hindering the delivery of essential nutrients and cells. Furthermore, chondrocytes, the specialized cells accountable for generating and preserving cartilage, exhibit a limited inherent capacity for proliferation and regeneration of impaired tissue. The limited regenerative capacity of cartilage poses a significant challenge in cases of cartilage injuries or degenerative conditions like osteoarthritis. Nanocomposites have been developed to provide a conducive environment for augmenting and facilitating the regeneration of cartilage. Through the integration of nanoparticles into scaffolds or delivery systems, nanocomposites offer a means to achieve accurate manipulation of the cellular microenvironment. Nanocomposites can provide a favorable mechanical microenvironment that is crucial for appropriate cellular responses and tissue development.

Our findings highlight the strategy to synthesize nanocomposites that possess excellent mechanical and crystalline properties, fostering rapid chondrogenesis. The results indicate that the mechanical properties of CQDs nanocomposite scaffolds can be tailored. The utilization of CQDs as a nanofiller resulted in a notable decline in the physical and mechanical properties, decreasing the crystallinity and Young's modulus of the hydrogels, while simultaneously enhancing its swelling ratio with an increase in the amount of CQDs. We observed a modest enhancement in pore size, porosity, and permeability of the CQDs, along with the attachment of hWJ-MSCs onto nanocomposites. The cytocompatibility of nanocomposites containing 0.1 % CQDs was found to be significantly higher than that of other groups which can be ascribed to the existence of a larger pore diameter and increased permeability. There was a positive correlation between the expansion of hWJ-MSCs and the percentage of CQDs used, especially in the later stages of the culture period. Moreover, an improvement in chondrocyte morphologies was observed in the nanocomposite scaffolds, in comparison to the TCP. The differentiation of hWJ-MSCs into chondrocytes over a period of 14 days is supposed to be influenced by three key factors of nanocomposites: crystallinity, stiffness, and pore size. Based on the nanofiller concentration, CNC-0.1 scaffolds exhibit an optimal mechanical properties and proliferation ability.

In conclusion, the limited regenerative capacity of cartilage can be addressed using nanocomposites, which is a promising approach for stimulating and guiding the repair process. Nanocomposites can be utilized as a versatile platform to improve cartilage regeneration through their controllable properties, mechanical reinforcement, and biocompatibility. The cues and support they offer are crucial for chondrocytes, overcoming the obstacles in cartilage regeneration and enabling the recovery of functional cartilage tissue.
